By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, SASK. - Richmond Sockeyes finally received the "fair shake" from the officiating that coach Orland Kurtenbach had been praying for Wednesday night. Sadly for the Sockeyes, that's the only item which went their way in a key Centennial Cup game against Humboldt Broncos. Richmond was thrashed 6-1 by the host team as the round-robin portion of the national junior A hockey tournament ended. The loss means the 'Eyes have to come back tonight and face Ontario's Pembroke Lumber Kings in the semi-final game. Humboldt advances to Saturday's winner-take-all final. The Broncos were 3-0 in the round-robin, Richmond 2-1, Pembroke 1-2 and Atlantic champion Dartmouth 0-3. "We have no excuses," said Kurtenbach. "'The refereeing was good. We just got outhustled, outskated, out everythinged. But it's over and done with now. We can only use it as a motivating tool. "Some nights it just doesn't go, and tonight was one of them.' Humboldt's Bill McDougall and Richmond's Stan Czenczek traded first power-play goals before the Broncos broke things open in the second. A

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - A psychological ploy used by Humboldt Broncos' coach Bernie Lynch has blown up in his face. The 33-year-old Lynch, in his fourth season with Broncos, unwittingly offended the entire community here when he ordered his players not to shake hands with Richmond Sockeyes following the Sockeyes' western final triumph last Wednesday. Lynch figured that since the Broncos and Sockeyes would be meeting again in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey tournament, they would save the hand-shaking for this week. "Richmond ain't won nothing yet," said Lynch at the time. But Lynch, who grew up mainly in southern Ontario, badly misjudged the sensibilities of the people in this farming community. He has been under heavy criticism since the perceived breach in hockey etiquette last week and in Wednesday's edition of the Humboldt Journal, a weekly newspaper, Lynch was roasted in the letters to the editor section.

By JACK WILSON of The Advocate Security will be tight tonight for the pivotal fifth game in the British Columbia-Alberta junior hockey series at the Red Deer Arena. Sgt. Bob Williams of city RCMP says recreation department officials met with police Monday to work out a plan to deal with any fights, on or off the ice, for the 7:30 contest between Richmond Sockeyes and Red Deer Rustlers.

However, he said he'd have a better idea this afternoon when general public advance tickets go on sale. The last two games in Red Deer between the Rustlers and Richmond Sockeyes have attracted about 1,650 people. In their Alberta championship series against St. Albert, home crowds hit the 2,290-capacity several times. Rustler general manager Graham Parsons has appealed to Red Deer fans to refrain from throwing debris onto the ice and at the Richmond players' bench.

the beginning of a race and seeing it through to the finish line. And that difference can mean a great deal to an injured athlete. "The basis of first aid is to do the first treatment and then get them off to a doctor and that's the end of your commitment. "But in sports. first aid, our commitment is to get to the person, do the first aid, and look towards putting the person back on the field as quickly as possible," explained Greg Moro, a 17-year veteran of sports first aid who is one of the instructors for the Sport Medicine Council of B.C.'s SportsAid program. *We look at the long term rather than the short term," he continued. "*A normal first aid person gets a person for a short period of time and they never see them again, where we have a person and we see them all the way through their recovery." Part of following an athlete's recovery is the sports aider recognizing his limitations and referring the injured person to the hospital, family doctor or physiotherapist. Moro added, "Sports first aiders know a bit more about how the body reacts to injury so we . . . know what to do along with the icing (of certain injuries) so that healing takes place. "I think we look at the overall picture where the first aid person just looks at the start of the picture." None of this, however, is meant to criticize conventional first aid practices, stresses Moro, who has an industrial first aid "A ticket" and practices conventional and sports first aid at the Exhibition Park race track in Vancouver. (He has also served as a trainer for the Vancouver Whitecaps, Nanaimo Clippers Junior A Tier 2 hockey team, this year's Centennial Cup Champion Richmond Sockeyes, last year's B.C. Winter Games, and the Mac's Super Series hockey tournament for the last three years; he was also the head trainer at UBC last year.) Moro said it should simply be recognized that people with conventional first aid training have limitations when it comes to sport-related injuries. He offered as an example a soccer tournament where both regular first aid attendants and sports aiders were on duty. During the course of a match, a player went down with a twisted ankle and was attended by both "aiders." The conventional first aid attendant recommended splinting the leg and sending the player to emergency. But the sports aider iced the injury and taped the ankle for support and the player went back into the match a little later. Ice is a key ingredient to immediate and long-term treatment of most sports-related injuries as part of the "RICE" system. That is, 1 rest, ice, compression and elevation. Rest simply means putting the injured part at rest while ice and elevation are used together for the same purpose. Icing the injured area constricts the flow of blood and, thus, decreases the injury bleeding into the area around it. And elevating the injury

Junior hockey has come such a long way here in the past year, it would be a shame to lose its gains in one short, ugly series. The Red Deer Rustlers dropped out of the league last season, and came back determined to sell the game as an inexpensive, fun family outing. They succeeded, on and off the ice. After a halting start, the team responded to the direction of a new coach and is making a serious run for the Canadian title. Inexpensive season tickets filled the stands with vocal, loyal fans. But in the series against the Richmond Sockeyes, some of those "fans" have turned ugly. They have littered the ice after every Red Deer goal. They have pelted the Richmond players with eggs, and tried to fight them. These yahoos can scarcely be called fans. They are ruining the game and seriously impairing the Rustlers' chances of winning this series. The Canadian Amateur Hockey Association simply won't put up with these antics. The association is acutely aware of junior hockey's bad image, and has taken steps to improve it. Fighting has been reduced and stickwork curbed. The association can dictate what's unaceptable on the ice; it has less control over what goes on in the stands. If the louts in the Red Deer Arena persist, it has few options. It can forfeit the series to Richmond, cancel it completely, or move the remaining games to a neutral site. All three options are no-win propositions for the Rustlers. The Rustlers really need those games here. The players 1 need their fans behind them to help them defeat a more experienced team. And Rustler management needs the home-gate revenue to pay its bills and carry on. Once the game begins, Rustler security staff is almost powerless to control the impulsive actions of individual fans. But Rustler management can and must take actions before the game to limit the chances of another fiasco. Security staff can screen customers carefully as they enter the Arena, to ensure vegetables or other projectiles don't get in. Tight screening like this is routine at rock concerts and other sporting events. The true Rustler fans won't object to the inconvenience, because they know what is at stake, and condemn the yahoo minority responsible for the problem. Rustler management can also make an announcement before the game, outlining the consequences if the ice is littered, or Richmond players are pelted. After that, it's up to the fans themselves. If they want to see the Rustlers win this series, if they want junior hockey to endure and prosper in Red Deer, they have to discipline themselves.

Centennial Cup champs HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo was named most valuable player in the four-team, round-robins series. British Columbia's Sockeyes skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2- 1 before Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond, which took seven of the game's 12 minor penalties. Curtis Chamberlin and Kevin Luke scored for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos.

(CP) The Richmond Sockeyes exploded for three goals in the third period to beat the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament. The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatche-. wan champion Humboldt Broncos. The other team in the championship event, the Dartmouth Fuel Kids from British Columbia, are also at 0-2. The teams that finish second and third in the round-robin portion of the tournament meet in a semifinal game on Thursday night. The winner of the semifinal tangles with the team that finishes first after 1 roundrobin play.

paragraphs.text: By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate RICHMOND, B.C. - Red Deer Rustlers may have dropped the first two games of their Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series to Richmond Sockeyes, but they're not returning home emptyhanded. Rustlers were at least able to grasp the confidence that they can indeed play with the large and extremely mobile Sockeyes, who tore to shreds their competition in the BCJHL playoffs before meeting Red Deer. After an embarrassing 11-4 setback the night before, the Rustlers were vastly improved Wednesday, leading 4-2 after two periods but eventually falling 6-4.

RED DEER, Alta. (CP) - Dixon Ward and Richard Anderson scored two goals each as the Red Deer Rustlers thumped the visiting Richmond Sockeyes 7-1 in a chippy B.C.-Alberta Centennial Cup hockey playoff game yesterday. Red Deer's triumph evened the best-ofseven series at 2-2. Saturday, the Rusters coralled the Sockeyes 6-3. Brian Puhalsky, Tracy Georgsen and Bob Duchak also scored for the Alberta Junior Hockey League champion Rustlers, who led 3-0 and 6-0 by periods. Dave Tomlinson had the only goal for the British Columbia Junior Hockey League champion Sockeyes. Red Deer outshot Richmond 46-34 and took 18 of the game's 28 minor penalties, 10 of 20 majors and 10 of 20 game misconducts. The teams meet again tomorrow night in Red Deer. Before, last weekend, the Sockeyes had gone undefeated in playoffs, including a 2-0 sweep of Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey Leaguechampion Quesnel Millionaires.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) The Pembroke Lumber Kings clinched a spot in the semi game of the Centennial junior A hockey championship tournament Tuesday night with an 8-4 victory over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids. The Ontario champions will meet the loser of today's final round-robin game between the Humboldt Broncos of Saskatchewan and the Richmond Sockeyes of British Columbia, who both have 2-0 records in the tournament.

How did the Red Deer Rustlers feel about going seven games with the Canadian junior hockey champions? Were the Richmond Sockeyes as tough a competition as they looked? Will the 1987-88 edition of the Rustlers go all the way this year? You can ask all these and other questions on last year's edition of the Rustlers or on the upcoming season, at Eleo's Soup and San at No. 178 5201 43rd St., just west of Red Deer Regional Hospital Centre. An Advocate advertising feature by JACQUELINE JANSEN Everyone's favorite players will be on hand at the restaurant from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. Thursday to get Red Deer in the mood for this year's hockey season and to promote a season ticket sales drive for the club. Season tickets can be purchased Thursday at the low price of $129 a pair. With 30 home games scheduled for the 1987-88 season, this works out to $2.15 per person per game. Where else can you find so much excitement and entertainment for so little?

After being cut, Hervey was considering calling it quits but after coming in contact with Orland Kurtenbach, a former National Hockey Leaguer with four teams over 11 seasons, Hervey's outlook on life changed for the better. Kurtenbach is the coach of the Richmond Sockeyes of the BCJHL. "Kurtenbach was a big influence on me. We had a talk about my off-ice attitude because I was letting a lot of little things bother me. "Playing under him really helped me out. He helped me make decisions,' continued Hervey. "'He said he'd get me a shot somewhere depending on how I played and we had a great year." In 17 regular season games, the hard-nosed blueliner had 24 points. The Sockeyes went on to win the Centennial Cup national tier 11 junior hockey championship at Humbolt. Sask., and Hervey was named to the first all-star team. "We had a great time. We had over ten, 20-year-olds and a lot of really good young guys with futures. We had five guys who had full rides to colleges back east," in the United States. Hervey, who couldn't stress enough that his favorite sport growing up was always hockey, pointed out that he prefers cool weather to the hot California sunshine and declared that to be successful, "it's real important to make hockey as fun as it can be and to have a really good work ethic." The 5', 11", 200-pounder, has brought his energetic a attitude to Moncton this season. He said he's, "still on cloud nine from the Winnipeg camp. (Jets' head coach) Dan Maloney was good to me and (general manager) John Ferguson was good to me." Hervey is presently on a 25- game amateur tryout and is waiting for a contract. He recently got word from the powers that be to get an apartment in Moncton, which means: a contract might be pending. He's been one of the steadiest defenceman for coach Rick Bowness this season, scoring one goal and assisting on two others and is second on the team in penalty minutes with 47. He's only played six games. "My game is to play physical, like clearing the front of •the net and going in the corners. I'm not a goon," he maintained. "And I'm an enforcer because I'm not a good enough fighter to be that...yet."

paragraphs.text: (Continued from 4a) muted to Langley to rebuild the team. The players followed and many expressed interest and the desire to work with this fa-. mous Coach. Muzz had taken Burnaby and Richmond to the top but could he take last place Langley to the top? joined the team executive. We decided to prepare a program but a late start and lack of support from the business community was disheartening. However, several sports fans took out ads and helped us put together a good program. Ironically, too many were more interested in how much business the ad would generate, than to see the community value, the support for the team and the goodwill for their business. We sought financial support from the Langley Township Council but they perceived the team as a "business" and denied d any support, did not grant concessions on ice rental, nor could we get much ice time to practice. Langley's team practiced in Richmond. We started to get some of the crowds back; some spirit was emerging in this first rebuilding year. Muzz Coached the team to 2nd place, a, wonderful achievement. Richmond Sockeyes went on to win B.C. championships, through to the Memorial Cup and we are sure their community support was paid off in aces, with the pride Richmond now has in their championship team. Muzz could do the same with Langley but the decision is now made to › abandon Langley which to those with pride in Langley and vision for the future will recognize is a true loss to the community. If Council could broaden their perspectives and recognize the team's potential to Langley, abandon the "face value" assumption that it is a commercial enterprise (technically it is so licenced but in reality it loses money), and exercise some influence over scheduling times to find a better and more harmonious balance, we would not be losing one of Langley's cherished possessions. So, where is the human cry from the Community? Apathy wins again. To many residents it will be no big deal: afterall, it's only a hockey team that

Red Deer Rustlers have a motto - 'Whatever it takes.' And that's what was required during the weekend as they posted back-to-back decisions over Richmond Sockeyes at the Arena, creating a 2-2 deadlock in the best-of-seven western Centennial Cup semifinal. Rustlers, with goaltender Ken Weiss turning in a magnificent performance, doubled the B.C. champions 6-3 Saturday, then zapped the visitors 7-1 Sunday in a chippy contest that featured 392 minutes in penalties, 221 to Richmond. The fifth game is Tuesday at 7:30 p.m. at the Arena. The weekend sweep was a complete turnaround from the first two games in Richmond, where Red Deer fell 11-4 and 6-4 and snapped a 17-game winning streak by the Sockeyes. "It took me one game to figure them out," smiled Rustler coach Larry Billows. "We had a couple of scouts tell us that Richmond would look strong the first game there, but that if we worked hard we could beat them in a best-of-seven series. "After watching them in Richmond, I had to agree. They're front-runners. I'm not predicting anything yet, but if they're going to win this series they're going to have to earn it. We're not going to let them waltz all the way to Humboldt (site of the 1987 Centennial Cup)." Sockeyes took the majority of the penalties Sunday out of frustration, based on bility to (1) solve netminder Weiss, and (2) manhandle the Rustlers. "We made good contact along the boards and in open ice," said Billows. "Richmond wasn't prepared for that. Maybe they don't hit like that in their league (BCJHL)." Brian Puhalsky, Tracy Georgsen and Rich Anderson - the later two on the power-play - staked Red Deer to a 3-0 lead and the hometown club, spurred on by 1,652 supporters, never looked back. Dixon Ward, Bob Duchak and Anderson doubled the Rustlers' margin in a wild second period that was delayed for 30 minutes by four on-ice battles, as well as a fracas behind the Richmond players bench that was instigated by a group of disorderly fans. Dave Tomlinson, with his fourth goal of the series, finally got the Sockeyes on the board at 1:39 of the third period, but Weiss shut the door the rest of the way.

By ELLIOTT PAP. Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, SASK. - Richmond Sockeyes finally received the "fair shake" from the officiating that coach Orland Kurtenbach had been praying for Wednesday night. Sadly for the Sockeyes, that's the only item which went their way in a key Centennial Cup game against Humboldt Broncos. Richmond was thrashed 6-1 by the host team as the round-robin portion of the national junior A hockey tournament ended. The loss means the 'Eyes have to come back tonight and face Ontario's Pembroke Lumber Kings in the semi-final game. Humboldt advances to Saturday's winner-take-all final. The Broncos were 3-0 in the round-robin, Richmond 2-1, Pembroke 1-2 and Atlantic champion Dartmouth 0-3. "We have no excuses," said Kurtenbach. "'The refereeing was good. We just got outhustled, outskated, out everythinged. But it's over and done with now. We can only use it as a motivating tool. "Some nights it just doesn't go, and tonight was one of them.' Humboldt's Bill McDougall and Richmond's Stan Czenezek traded first-period power-play goals before the Broncos broke things open in the second.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - A psychological ploy used by Humboldt Broncos' coach Bernie Lynch has blown up in his face. The 33-year-old Lynch, in his fourth season with the Broncos, unwittingly offended the entire community here when he ordered his players not to shake hands with Richmond Sockeyes following the Sockeyes' western final triumph last Wednesday. Lynch figured that since the Broncos and Sockeyes would be meeting again in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey tournament, they would save the hand-shaking for this week. "Richmond ain't won nothing yet," said Lynch at the time. But Lynch, who grew up mainly in southern Ontario, badly misjudged the sensibilities of the people in this farming community. has been under heavy criticism since the perceived breach in hockey etiquette last week and in Wednesday's edition of the Humboldt Journal, a weekly newspaper, Lynch was roasted in the letters to the editor section

By ELLIOTT PAP The 'Eyes have it. Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes collected their first B.C. Junior Hockey League championship in eight years Tuesday night, edging Kelowna Packers 3-2 at Richmond Arena to sweep the bestof-nine final five straight. The Sockeyes, who are loaded with major junior veterans, made it look easy as they romped to 13 consecutive wins in three BCJHL series. "I must admit that is a surprise," said Kurtenbach, the former Vancouver Canuck captain and coach. "'You just never know what the fickle finger of fate will do. But we seemed to have it in gear. I'm very honored and very happy to be a winner my first year coaching in junior." The Sockeyes' season is far from over, however. As a tier two t team, their particular dream goes all the way to the Centennial Cup national championship.

CRAIG HODGE ALL SMILES after sweeping Kelowna, Richmond Sockeyes' captain teammate Dean Rutledge (20). Also joining the celebration is Rob Trevor Dickie (left) hoists B.C. Junior League trophy along with Sumner (right). Sockeyes went through playoffs without a loss.

Sockeyes win Centennial Cup HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) - Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo was named most valuable player in the four-team, roundrobin series. . British Columbia's Sockeyes skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2-1 before Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond, which took seven of the game's 12 minor penalties. Curtis Chamberlin and Kevin Luke scored for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos.

paragraphs.text: An all-Spokane officiating crew will work the exhibition game between the U.S. and Canadian Olympic teams Nov. 28 in Tacoma. Dennis LaRue, the lone American selected to referee in the 1988 Olympics, will be the referee in the Tacoma Dome. His linesmen will be Kerry Blair and Cory Bowles. All three formerly played Junior B hockey in Spokane, and LaRue played one year of senior amateur. LaRue and Blair also will work the U.S.-Canada game Nov. 27 in Los Angeles. Mark Rudolph, officiating program director of the Amateur Hockey Association of the U.S., said LaRue may receive other exhibition assignments. -Don Cherry, who played pro hockey for the 1962-63 Spokane Comets, has been inducted into the Rochester (N.Y.) Americans Hall of Fame. Cherry played and coached for Rochester in the pro American Hockey League after leaving Spokane. He later coached in the National Hockey League. Joe Crozier, another former Comet defenseman, NHL coach and Rochester coach, was one of the e original inductees in the Americans' Hall of Fame last year. I Dan Holden, the only local player ever to play for the Spokane Chiefs in the Western Hockey League, is playing for Acadia University in Wolfville, Nova Scotia. Holden played at the University of Calgary in 1991-85, then joined the Chiefs the following season. Former Chief defenseman Rick Herbert, who holds the WHL record of 329 games played, has joined the Richmond Sockeyes of the Tier Two British Columbia Junior Hockey League. Marc Pezzin, who coached Herbert in Spokane and Brandon, said Herbert was not interested in playing a sixth season in the after being released by the Chicago Blackhawks. Herbert plans to play college hockey in Canada next year, Pezzin said.

The former Langley Eagles have finally landed at a new home - and it will be Chilliwack, not Richmond as first proposed. After hearing strenuous objections to their proposed move to Richmond, the B.C. Junior Hockey League team finalized a deal on Friday, June 12, that will see the club settle in the Fraser Valley City. Eagles had severed their longtime relationship with Langley last month, giving up their ice time at the Langley Civic Centre when no satisfactory practice times could be negotiated. But a proposal to move to Seafair Arena in Richmond met with stern disapproval from the rival Richmond Sockeyes, who play out of Richmond Arena. "We thought we could talk Richmond (Sockeyes) into setting up a rivalry, but they would have no part of it," said Eagles coach and general manager Buzz MacPherson. The new team, to be called the Chilliwack Eagles, has received approval from the league and Chilliwack Council. It will play out of Twin Rinks Arena in Sardis, one mile south of Chilliwack. "We regret moving out of Langley, but the Civic Centre couldn't accommodate us," said MacPherson. Prime icetime in the Chilliwack Coliseum has already been allocated to the still-un-named Chilliwack entry in the Pacific Northwest Hockey League. In other related news, Chilliwack's Dave Archibald, who scored 107 points for the Portland Winter Hawks of the Western Hockey League last season and was named the WHL's most sportsmanlike player, was drafted sixth overall in the National Hockey League's amateur draft by the Minnesota North Stars and Langley forward Neil Eisenhut1 was selected in the twelfth round by the Vancouver Canucks.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Mike Gaber scored at 3:31 of overtime Tuesday night to give the Humboldt Broncos a 4-3 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes and new life in their Centennial Cup playoff series. The victory forced a seventh and deciding game in the Western Canada final here Wednesday night. Bill McDougall led the Broncos with two goals and Curtis Chamberlin was the other marksman for Humboldt which trailed 1-0 at the end of the first period and was tied 2-2 entering the third period. Paul Rutherford led the Sockeyes with two goals while Dave Tomlinson added the other goal for Richmond which was outshot 46-45. Humboldt, which got spectacular netminding from Bruce Hoffort who made 42 saves, scored twice on the power play while Richmond counted once with the man advantage. There were two key mistakes Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach felt gave Humboldt the victory.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) - The Richmond Sockeyes exploded for three goals in the third period to beat the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament. The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the roundrobin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champions, Humboldt Broncos. It was the second straight loss for the Lumber Kings, Ontario's representatives in the four-team tournament. Dartmouth Fuel Kids from British Columbia, the other team in the tournament, are also 0-2. Dave Tomlinson led Richmond with two goals, while Brian Kozak and Stan Czenczek scored one each. Czenczek scored the winning goal at 6:35 of the third period, after his team held a 1-0 lead through the first two frames.

Abbotsford Falcons' new coach and general manager Gary McCaskill has lost some sleep this week, after seeing his team play poorly i in both of their first two games in the B.C. Junior Hockey League season. McCaskill may have lost sleep. One player - Ryan Seidler - lost his job, more cuts are coming say the coach, and veteran Kirk Phare was traded to Richmond Sockeyes for cash. But the player move McCaskill would most like to see happen is Brad Smiley's, either back into a Falcon uniform, or - into another BCJHL uniform, which would bring some player help to Abbotsford. Smiley demanded a trade to Penticton - and Penticton only - late in the pre-season, but as the Knights know they have McCaskill pinned into a corner on that one, have not offered much at all for the player who led the Falcons in scoring last season. "I'm frustrated by the whole thing," McCaskill said Wednesday. "It's simply still in limbo." Smiley's worth is known around the league. Four other teams - Merritt, Summerland, Vernon and Delta - have made e McCaskill offers for his disgruntled scorer, any y of which McCaskill said he was willing to accept. By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter For the 1986-87 Richmond Sockeyes, the Centennial Cup championship is now just a memory. For the bulk of the team, Richmond will soon be just a memory, too. Of this year's roster, only 16-year-olds Rob Sumner and Jason Talo appear set to return to the Sockeyes, who won B.C.'s second consecutive Canadian junior A championship by downing the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 Saturday. The nine 20-year-olds forming the experienced core of the team are no longer eligible. That includes standouts like goaltender Frank Romeo, the Centennial Cup's most valuable player, and defenceman Matt Hervey, who had a tryout with the Los Angeles Kings last year. Sockeyes' president Horst Wilkomm is thinking about taking a break from hockey and even coach Orland Kurtenbach must decide his future. Humboldt will also be the scene of mass departures. In addition to several players taking U.S. college scholarships, the Broncos will lose their seven 20-year-olds and maybe head coach Bernie Lynch. Lynch, also the Saskatchewan champions' general manager, raised the ire of Humboldt fans following the Broncos' refusal to shake hands with the Sockeyes when Richmond won the Western Canadian. championship. The incident provoked letters of outrage in the weekly Humboldt Journal and much muttering among the Uniplex crowds.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Matt Hervey scored two goals Sunday night to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to a 5- 3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in Centennial Cup hockey playoff action. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven Western Canada final. The sixth game is scheduled here Tuesday night. Humboldt led 2-0 after after the first period but Richmond tied the game 2- 2 after the middle frame. Brian Kozak, Dave Tomlinson and Dave Rutherford added goals for Richmond. Bill McDougall scored two goals for Humboldt while Garnet Kazuik completed the scoring for the Saskatchewan club. Goalie Frank Romeo made 30 saves for Richmond while Bruce Hoffort blocked 22 shots for Humboldt. The Broncos' tight-checking game stymied the Sockeyes for the first half of the game, the same tactic the visitors employed in a 2-1 victory Saturday night. **They had us going the same way in the first period and a half tonight,'

Sun News Services RED DEER, Alta. - Red Deer Rustlers beat the Richmond Sockeyes 7-5 in overtime Tuesday in a Centennial Cup playoff hockey game that saw no repeat of the unruly fan behavior that marred Sunday's match. The victory enabled the Rustlers to take a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven tier two junior western semi-final series. The sixth game goes here tonight. "There wasn't a bit of trouble," said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach. "The management asked the fans to refrain from throwing anything on the ice and there was no problem." Dixon Ward's goal 59 seconds into the 10-minute overtime period was the winner. Geoff Gosselin added an insurance marker at 9:50 into an empty net just after Sockeye Jason Phillips missed a penalty shot. The Sockyes were led by Brian Kozak, who had two goals, while Paul Rutherford, Mike Claringbull and Phillips contributed singles. The Rustlers held period leads of 3-2 and 4-3, fell behind 5-4 in the third period but managed to pull even at 13:16 to force the overtime. Richard Anderson and Brian Puhalsky each scored twice for Red Deer and Greg Pulliam added a single.

The banquet was as impressive as always when the federal government is treating. Ben Johnson, Todd Brooker and Carolyn Waldo, the cream of our athletes, were served up on the half shell along with someone named Doug Mitchell, who is not the commissioner of the Canadian Football League. Mitchell is a 32-year-old school teacher who was honored because he pulled his junior hockey team out of a playoff series in Ontario last month. For this he received the r Fair Play Award, warm words from sports minister Otto Jelinek and a standing ovation. When you hear of the Animal House ARCHIE behavior un- McDONALD dermining the junior series between Red Deer and Richmond Sockeyes, when you read of nine American League players being ejected because of a brawl in the warmup, when you see the Canadian and Soviet juniors end in a war, the Fair Play award is like hockey's Nobel Peace Prize. Mitchell is a hockey idealist. He played four years at University of Waterloo and then five years of Intermediate A. When pressed, he would get involved in fisticuffs because that is the way the game has been accepted. But when he thought about it, he concluded, "What the hell is fighting doing in hockey anyway? When you pose that question to yourself, it is ridiculous. Fighting is illegal, yet it is allowed to happen." Last fall, when he became coach of Port Elgin Bears, a j junior C team, he put some of his ideals into practice. If players fought, they were suspended by the team. They had to meet dress standards off the ice. In time, their games became cleaner. When opponents realized that the Bears didn't fight, an intimidation factor was withdrawn. If an opponent started a battle, he would be hit with an aggressor

MONTREAL (CP) - The 1987 Capitcher, Grand Prix race in Montreal is dead, the victim of a bitter brewery battle. Roger Dore of Labatt Breweries Ltd.® announced Monday that atAtlanta to hold the race, set for June 14, have failed. Labatt, sponsor of the event for the last nine years, fought Molson Breweries Ltd., which wanted to run the race, in the courts, accusing its riGiants of unethical behavior. The uncertainty prompted the Federation international du sport automobile to remove the race from the Formula One calendar last month. But Labatt insisted it could overcome the problems which it claimed were due to foul play on the part of Molson, promoter Jack Long, the City of Montreal and the Formula One Constructors Association, which controls the drivers and their sponsors. Admitting defeat for this year, Dore told reporters Labatt "will continue working to have the Grand Prix Labatt reinstated "for next year for years after that.' Sockeyes sock it to Kings HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) The Richmond Sockeyes scored four goals in the third period to beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night in the Centennial Cup Junior A hockey championship tournament. The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the roundrobin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champions, Humboldt Broncos. It was the second straight loss for the Lumber Kings, Ontario's representatives in the four-team tournament.

Swedes the gold medal, it also gave Canada a favorable draw for the Sockeyes win 2nd in Centennial HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) The Richmond Sockeyes exploded for three goals in the third period to beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament. The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos. It was the second straight loss for the Lumber Kings, the Ontario representatives in the fourteam tournament. The other team in the championship event, the Dartmouth Fuel Kids from British Columbia, are also at 0-2. The teams that finish second and third in the round-robin portion of the tournament meet in a semi-final game on Thursday night. The winner of the semifinal tangles with the team that finishes first after round-robin play. Dave Tomlinson led Richmond with two goals, while Brian Kozak and Stan Czenczek had one each. Czenczek scored the winning goal at 6:35 of the third period, after his team held a 1-0 lead through the first two frames. George Dupont scored for the Lumber Kings, who were outshot 26-25. The Sockeyes were assessed 11 of 15 minor penalties. An all-Spokane officiating crew will work the exhibition game between the U.S. and Canadian Olympic teams Nov. 28 in Tacoma. Dennis LaRue, the lone American selected to referee in the 1988 Olympics, will be the referee in the Tacoma Dome. His linesmen will be Kerry Blair and Cory Bowles. All three formerly played Junior B hockey in Spokane, and LaRue played one year of senior amateur. LaRue and Blair also will work the U.S.-Canada game Nov. 27 in Los Angeles. Mark Rudolph, officiating program director of the Amateur Hockey Association of the U.S., said LaRue may receive other exhibition assignments. Don Cherry, who played pro hockey for the 1962-63 Spokane Comets, has been inducted into the Rochester (N.Y.) Americans Hall of Fame. Cherry played and coached for Rochester in the pro American League after leaving Spokane. He later coached in the National Hockey League. Joe Crozier, another former Comet defenseman, NHL coach and Rochester coach, was one of the original inductees in the Americans' Hall of Fame last year. I Dan Holden, the only local player ever to play for the Spokane Chiefs in the Western Hockey League, is playing for Acadia University in Wolfville, Nova Scotia. Holden played at the University of Calgary in 1984-85, then joined the Chiefs the following season. Former Chief defenseman Rick Herbert, who holds the WHL record of 329. games played, has joined the Richmond Sockeyes of the Tier Two British Columbia Junior Hockey League. Marc Pezzin, who' coached Herbert in Spokane and Brandon, said Herbert was not interested in playing a sixth season in the WHL after being released by the Chicago Blackhawks. Herbert plans to play college hockey in Canada next year, Pezzin said.

two and a half seasons with the Kings. "I learned a lot from (Kings coach) Al Ferchuk . . he's a very good technical coach," said Pulliam, who joined the Rustlers in early January. Last season, Pulliam was the Kings' captain. He moved into that role with the Rustlers in mid-February, shortly after Billows moved behind the Red Deer bench. "It was a joint decision by Larry and the players, who responded well to me right off the bat," explained Pulliam, who replaced Ron Lebsack as the Rustler captain. "It wasn't hard to adjust to the role because I'd been in this position before with the Kings. "My job is to help get the guys ready, to help instill confidence in each and every player." Billows admitted he's happy to have Pulliam's leadership abilities. "He's good in the dressing room," said the Rustler coach. "We have three of four inspirational guys and Pully is one of them. We have to rely on guys like that now." Tonight, Pulliam will be called on to deliver the most important pre-game pep talk of his career. "I'll remind them that they all have a job, and if they're willing to work 100 per cent as a team, we'll be successful," he said. Saints assistant coach Chris Stewart expects his team to turn in its best effort of the series tonight. "Since the Rustlers beat us at home in the first game, we've been in the situation of having to win one in Red Deer." said Stewart. "We still haven't done it, and obviously we have to Friday. Either we'll lose in six or win in seven (at home).' "The guys are loose we're ready," countered Billows. "It will be a case of who wants it the most and we certainly want it." By MONTE STEWART Herald-Tribune staff The acronym for the B.C. Junior Hockey League is BCJHL. It is an abbreviation which can now afford an extra "C' for r champion. Richmond Sockeyes rendered the Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey League still winless in Centennial Cup playoffs by sweeping Quesnel Millionaires in a best-of-three B.C. Tier II championship series last weekend. (If Grande Prairie North Stars had defeated Quesnel in the PCJHL finals, they also would have played for the B.C. championship because the league is sanctioned by the B.C. Amateur Hockey Association.) The Sockeyes drubbed the Millionaires 5-1 in Richmond on Saturday night. Friday, the hosts edged the PCJHL champion Quesnel 4-3. Richmond now advances to meet Red Deer Rustlers in the next round of the Centennial Cup playoffs. The Rustlers locked up the Alberta Junior Hockey League title with an 8-5 triumph over St. Albert Saints on Friday night. Red Deer took the best-of-seven AJHL final 4-2. With the victories over the Millionaires, the Sockeyes remained undefeated in the playoffs. Their post-season mark now stands at 16-0. The BCJHL-AJHL showdown will be a best-of-seven affair. The first two games will be held in Richmond and the remaining games - depending on how many are necessary - will be held in Red Deer. Although the Mills came close Friday, the PCJHL champions haven't won a single game against BCJHL clubs in Centennial Cup playoffs. Penticton Knights blanked Prince George Spruce Kings 2-0 in last year's best-of-three series and went on to win the Centennial Cup.

Clippers 4, Sockeyes 7 By MICHAEL RHODE For The Daily Free Press RICHMOND For 60 minutes Tuesday night, Nanaimo Clippers played Richmond Sockeyes dead even. The Clippers skated, hit and hustled stride for stride with the Sockeyes before dropping a 7-4 overtime decision in the opening game of the Coastal Division bestof-seven semi-final playoff series. Game two in the B.C. Junior Hockey League series goes tonight in Richmond. To say the Clippers played their best road game of the year in Richmond would be an understatement. Nanaimo controlled the play on several occasions, but had trouble beating Richmond goalie Frank Romeo. "This is the best we've played on the road since beginngame, ing of the year," said Clipper assistant coach Mickey Willgress. "I don't think anybody had a weak game.' Brian Kozak's second goal of the game at 3:28 of the 10-minute overtime session proved to be the win-

/ have to disagree with my colleague Al Cameron, who last week predicted Nanaimo Clippers would win just one game in their best-of-seven playoff series with the powerful Richmond Sockeyes. Before Tuesday night's overtime Clipper loss Tuesday night in Richmond, I told Cameron the Clippers would win both this weekend's home games before falling in six games.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP.) - The Humboldt Broncos won a place in the final of the Centennial Cup Junior A hockey championship tournament Wednesday night with a 6-1 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes. The losers, with a round-robin record of 2-1, will meet the Pembroke Lumber Kings in tonight's semifinal.

Anybody out there got a map to lend Trevor Hendry? "I'm still not sure where I am," says the newest member of the Fort McMurray Oil Barons. Hailing from the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Richmond Sockeyes, the talented left winger is sure of one thing - he's a lot closer to the Arctic Ocean than the Pacific. Still, he admits Fort McMurray has been a pleasant surprise so far. "I guess it's alright out here. The Barons organization has treated me really well, and I'm used to the (long) bus trips because I spent three-and-ahalf years in the (Western Hockey League)." Fort McMurray acquired the sixfoot-two, 185-pound winger in a deal for Dean Rutledge, the Alberta Junior Hockey League's third leading scorer who decided to finish his Junior career in Richmond. So far, the Barons seem to be profiting from the trade. In his first two games before a hometown crowd, Hendry scored five times and added three assists against the Hobbema Hawks last weekend. Playing on a line with Brian Shantz at centre and Barry Kress on right wing, Hendry looked like he'd been with the two all season. "Shantz is young (16) and has good speed a and everything. He's going to be a hell of a player. (Kress) is the kind of guy that can do everything. He can score he can handle the puck

B.C. Junior Hockey League All-Star Game at Nicola Valley Memorial Arena in Merritt. Forwards Darren Biggs and Mike Knapton will join defenceman Grant McDonald on the Coastal Division All-Stars, team coached by Richmond Sockeyes' coach Orland Kurtenbach. Clipper blueliner Sean Stotts did not travel to Merritt Wednesday night due to an injured ankle suffered in Nanaimo's 6-2 win over Delta Flyers Tuesday night. He will likely play for the Clippers Friday night at home against Richmond. Stotts' place on the team will be taken by either Brad Pascal of Langley Eagles or Rod Sumner of Richmond. The Interior All-Stars, coached by Bill Hogaboam of Kelowna Packers, won last year's game in Nanaimo. Meanwhile, Rob Clarke had his fourth three goal night in as many games the Richmond Sockeyes

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) - Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to an easy 7- 3 win over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Sunday. It was the second loss in two days for Dartmouth. The Nova Scotians opened the week-long, round-robin tournament Saturday with a 7-2 defeat at the hands of the host Humboldt Broncos. Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson and Matt Hervey also scored for the Sockeyes, who piled up 44 shots against Dartmouth's 29. Richmond zipped to a 3-1 lead in the first period and built the margin to 6- 2 in the second.

by Larry Pruner Suddenly, the Richmond Sockeyes are playing darn good hockey. And on Wednesday night in Richmond, they did it at the Delta Flyers' expense. The defending Centennial Cup champions broke open a 2-2 tie in the second period with five unanswered goals and breezed to a 7-2 thrashing of the Flyers in B.C. Junior Hockey League play. The loss dropped the Flyers' first-place record to 23-6 on the season, while the Sockeyes moved into second spot 11 points behind the coastal division leaders. "They beat us handily," conceded Flyers' general manager and coach Rick Hopper. "They have a whole new line-up. There's not going to be another easy game against the Richmond Sockeyes."

After a valiant season; struggling from behind time after time to eventually win the Peace- Cariboo Junior Hockey League title, the Quesnel Millionaire's fell short in their bid for the Mowat Cup, the pinnacle of success in B.C. Tier II Junior hockey, to the Richmond Sockeyes.

DAVE PALLER PHOTO Abbotsford Falcons' forward Mike Paller moved in all alone on Nanaimo Clippers' Wade Flaherty last week, but the nifty netminder won this showdown. Clippers scored at the 17-second mark of sudden death overtime to trim the Falcons 4-3 in B.C. Junior Hockey League action at MSA Arena. Falcons host Richmond Sockeyes tonight (Saturday) at 8 p.m.

RED DEER. Alta. - The Richmond Sockeyes scored five first-period goals and coasted to a 7-3 victory over the Red Deer Rustlers to square their western Centennial Cup semifinal at three wins each last night. The Sockeyes, British Columbia Junior Hockey League champions, led 5-1 after the first period and 5-3 after the second. The Sockeyes outshot the Rustlers, Alberta Junior Hockey League champions, 36-33. Rob Clarke scored three times for Richmond and Tony Bobbitt, Dave Tomlinson, Brian Kozak and Mike McCormick added single goals. Pete DeGraaf with two goals and Dixon Ward replied for Red Deer. The Rustlers took 18 of 31 minor penalties and two misconducts. The deciding game in the series will be played tonight in Red Deer.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Tony Bobbitt's goal at 1:04 of a 10-minute overtime period gave the Richmond Sockeyes a 4-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in a Centennial Cup junior hockey playoff game yesterday night. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 2-1 lead in the best-of-seven Tier 2 western final series. The fourth game will be played in Richmond Saturday. Dave Tomlinson, Mike Claringbull and Rob Clarke scored the other goals for the Sockeyes, who were outshot 39- 23 and trailed 1-0 after the first period and 2-1 after the second. Bill McDougall had two goals and Curtis Chamberlin one for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos.

The Rustlers embarrassed themselves, their coach and their league Tuesday night, barely showing up in an 11-4 loss to Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes. "That's the worst game I've ever seen us play," muttered Red Deer coach Larry Billows, who took over the club in February. "Our defence stunk and our goaltender (Glen Furgason) played probably his worst game. We weren't skating, we were floating. We didn't play with any aggression. On their last two goals, we just gave up." The only consolation for Red Deer is the fact this Centennial Cup western semi-final series is a best-of-seven, not total goals. Richmond now leads it 1-0 with game two tonight, 7:30, at Richmond Arena. "I thought they played tougher hockey out Alberta," said Sockeye goaltender Frank Romeo. RICHMOND, B.C. Any hockey team that wins 17 consecutive post-season games, a feat Richmond Sockeyes have now accomplished, requires more than its share of breaks. Sockeyes got a large dose of good fortune with 13 minutes remaining in Wednesday's Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal game against Red Deer Rustlers at the Richmond Arena. Trailing 4-3, Richmond was handed a five-minute power play when Rustler defenceman Keith Lightbown was ejected for pummelling Sockeye forward Dave Tomlinson, who never removed his gloves. To that point, the Rustlers had used the splendid goaltending of pick-up Ken Weiss and a disciplined defence to keep the explosive Sockeyes at bay. It was far and away a superior effort to the one they turned in Tuesday, when they were hammered 11-4 in the opener of the best-of-seven set. But killing a five-minute penalty without the Sockeyes inflicting massive damage was simply too much to ask. Richmond scored three goals while Lightbown was removing his equipment and pulled out a 6-4 victory. Sockeyes now lead the series 2-0, but will have to win it in Red Deer, where the remainder of the games will be played. The third game is Saturday at 8 p.m. and the fourth Sunday at 7 p.m. "We had a game plan and stuck to it for two periods, but then we had a little misfortune with the referee and a little misfortune with one of our players," said an angry Rustler coach Larry Billows. "That was a dumb play by Lightbown. I don't know what he was thinking of." Dean Rutledge, who started the season with Fort McMurray Oil Barons, drew the Sockeyes even at 9:52, defenceman Matt Hervey notched the winner his third of the game at 11:19, and Brian Kozak added some insurance 88 seconds later. "We took some dumb penalties tonight as well, so I guess they evened out," offered Richmond general manager-coach Orland Kurtenbach, who stressed his club had to work extra hard to pull out the victory. "It didn't come easy for us," he stated. "We had 36 shots in the first two periods and weren't getting the puck past their goaltender (Weiss). He played a fine game for Red Deer, he really had us frustrated." Red Deer winger Dixon Ward opened the scoring midway through the first period and Hervey tied it with a late power-

RICHMOND. B.C. (CP) - Matt Hervey scored two goals yesterday night to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to a 5-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in Centennial Cup hockey playoff action. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven Western Canada final. The sixth game is scheduled here Tuesday night. Humboldt led 2-0 after after the first period but Richmond tied the game 2-2 after the middle frame. Brian Kozak, Dave Tomlinson and Dave Rutherford added goals for Richmond. Bill McDougall scored two goals for Humboldt while Garnet Kazuik completed the scoring for the Saskatchewan club.

S-P Services It was a series decided as much by timely bounces as tight checking, and the Richmond Sockeyes got the last bounce at the right time. Richmond's Dave Tomlinson scored with 2:37 remaining in regulation time Wednesday night to snap a 4-4 tie and give Richmond a 5-4 victory visiting Humboldt Broncos to thin the Abbott Cup. Sockeyes won the bestof-seven Western .Canada Tier Two junior hockey series four games to three. Richmond and Humboldt each won two one-goal games in a series that saw the Sockeyes outscore the Broncos 26-24. "Our guys played super hockey here," said Humboldt coach Bernie Lynch. "I can't ask them to play better than they did. But the way the puck seemed to find a way to jump over our sticks, it just seemed that somebody up there said, 'Sorry, but you're not going to win tonight.' "I told them after the game to remember that this wasn't a bestof-seven, it's a best-of-nine. If we' beat them in the Centennial Cup, nobody will remember who the Western champion was."

There are two things the Humboldt Broncos do very consistently: one is win hockey games, the other is make money. Although they failed to accom-' plish the former on Wednesday, losing 5-4 to the Richmond Sockeyes in the seventh game of their Abbott Cup series in Richmond, they almost certainly will do the latter with the Centennial Cup tournament which officially opens in Humboldt on Friday. The Broncos, who turned smalltime hockey drafts into a bigleague operation, have taken the Centennial Cup tournament and turned it into another winner. With Cup tickets going as an eight-game package for $50, the team managed t to virtually sell out the 2,400-seat Uniplex. Add to that the contributions of eight or nine sponsors, including the Bauer skate company, and the Broncos were looking at adding to their already impressive bank account. And all this was done with the noblest of intentions. "It wasn't set up to make mon-
by Russ Ullyot While hockey fans will be watching to see if the Coastal Division Richmond Sockeyes can repeat as Centennial Cup champions, the teams that may bear the most attention could be

by Russ Ullyot An old shootout got the B.C. Junior Hockey League season started at the Chilliwack Coliseum on Friday. But when the smoke cleared, the Chilliwack Eagles fluttered to the ground and the visiting Richmond Sockeyes escaped town with an 8-6 victory. The opening game in the 1987-88 BCJHL season saw two teams of opposite character go at each other. The Sockeyes' skaters had the upper hand over the grinding style of the Eagles. On the bright side, Darryl Anderson notched a hat-trick for the Eagles with other markers coming off the sticks of Greg Anderson, Mike Dutton and Cam Matches. For Richmond, Brian McFarlane had a five-point night, scoring twice and adding three assists. Mario Desjardins notched two goals, with singles to Scott Miller, John Atkinson and Tony Bobbitt. Richmond outshot Chilliwack 43-25. The Sockeyes were decididly out-hit by the Eagles. But Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach countered, "'We outshot them." Kurtenbach added, "It's not the kind of game you like to see, and you only hope you're on the right end of the score at the end." The Eagles held a 4-3 lead after the first 20 minutes. Darryl Anderson opened the scoring on a three-way passing play from Jim Butterworth and Derby Bognar that gave him a wide open net in which to slip the puck. Richmond scored the only two second-period markers to take a 5-4 lead, and the Sockeyes added a power play marker and a short-handed goal in the third en route to the win. The Eagles were held to two power play opportunities, while giving the Sockeyes three chances with the man advantage. Quill Notes Chilliwack minor hockey was well represented in the opening-day lineup for the Eagles. In uniform for Chilliwack were Bart McAstocker, Jerry Lund, Bryan Padgham and Marc Gaultier. Padgham and Gaultier each picked up one assist in the contest. Padgham also had three minor penalties, while Gaultier handled himself well in the game's only fight late in the third period. Unfortunately for Lund, the game ended abruptly midway through the third period when he went down in a collision at centre ice. Lund will be out two weeks with strained knee ligaments. The Eagles' next games are on home ice Friday and Saturday (Sept. 25 and 26). On Friday, the Eagles will play at the Coliseum against the Summerland Buckaroos before shifting to Vedder Twin Rinks on Saturday to host the Abbotsford Falcons.

The top candidate for the job is said to be Jim Farelli, who last "season coached Pembroke Lumber Kings of the Central Junior 'Hockey League to the semifinals of the Centennial Cup, Canada's Tier II junior championship. Lumber Kings, who won one of three games in the four-team preliminary round-robin portion of the tournament, were eliminated in semifinal play by (eventual champion Richmond *Sockeyes. Farelli is vacationing at his cottage near Pettawawa. Gord Wood, Canadian director of hockey operations, is also out of town this week. Farelli is a former coach in the OHL with Sault Ste. Greyhounds during parts of the 1973-74 and 1974-75 seasons when his son Carey was a star with the club. Farelli took over Greyhounds from Abbie Carricato after Carricato resigned in late November of 1973 with Greyhounds sitting seventh in the 10-team loop with a 10-15-2 won-lost-tied record. Sault Ste. Marie completed the season in ninth place with a 24- 40-6 record. The next season, Greyhounds struggled again and Farelli was replaced late in the season. Greyhounds, with a 25-36-9 record, finished 10th with 59 points, just one point behind Canadians, who occupied the eighth and final playoff spot that season, and tied with London Knights. London was awarded ninth place after having won more games than Greyhounds. Canadians open their OHL training camp on Sept.2. The club's regular season begins on Sept. 25 against Sault Ste. Marie.

Staff Writer If the story sounds a bit familiar, don't be surprised. For two periods, Nanaimo Clippers matched Richmond Sockeyes evenly. The locals hit, skated and scored as well as the Coastal Division regu ular season champions. ½ But, as was the case in games one and two in Richmond, the Sockeyes showed their experience and pulled out a 7-3 win over Nanaimo in game three of their B.C. Junior Hockey League best of seven semi-final series Friday at Civic Arena before a season high 1,415 fans. The win gives Richmond a 3-0 lead, with game four scheduled for tonight at Civic Arena at 8. The Clippers held a 3-2 lead going into the third period on the strength of a last-second goal by Corky Parkes. Richmond disputed the goal, resulting in captain Trevor Dickie's ejection from the game. The goal stood, and it seemed the marker would give Nanaimo the momentum together a good third period® together and finally beat the Sockeyes. However Rob Clarke's first goal of the series at 1:43 of the third tied the score with the Sockeyes enjoying a man advantage. A little over a minute later, Jason Phillips, with his second of the game, put the Sockeyes up for good, breaking in alone on Flaherty and deking him out to score into a wide open net. Dave Tomlinson, Mike McCormick and Dean Rutledge with an empty-netter broke the game open in the third. Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach says his team was able to withstand pressure of Parkes' late goal in the second. "I just told the guys that we had been having everything work against us," said Kurtenbach. "We had to keep our cool. Once we got the tying goal, we just started to roll. Clipper captain Sean Stotts says third-period letdowns are nothing new for Nanaimo. "It's been happening that way all year long," said Stotts. "We're just not coming out mentally prepared for the third period. We have to play the same way we do in the first two." Nanaimo goaltender Wade Flaherty, who played another outstanding game for Nanaimo, feels his team got a bum rap from referee Dave Dodson. "We came out of the dressing room ready, but the ref made a bad call on Hodgins and that hurt us when they scored the powerplay goal," said Flaherty, who faced 52 Sockeye shots. "They got another one really quick, and it kind of put us down.' Clipper coach Rich Dutka says his team deserved a better fate. "In the first five minutes (of the third) they scored three goals, and

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Humboldt Broncos beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-2 Sunday night to take an early lead in the roundrobin Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship. Humboldt also won the tournament opener, beating Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7-2 Saturday night. The Fuel Kids, crowd favorites in the early going, fell 7-3 to Richmond Sockeyes in the only other game Sunday. The four-team tournament ends Saturday. The hometown Broncos built a 3- 1 lead in the first period and hung on for the win after the Lumber Kings battled back to make it 3-2 in the second.

NOTEBOOK: OK: Brophy rewarded From The Whig- Standard wire services A year ago, coach Dan Maloney took the underdog Toronto Maple Leafs to the deciding game of the Norris Division final. Leaf owner Harold Ballard was initially impressed by his team's performance, but then offered his coach a new contract without a substantial raise. Maloney considered the incident an insult, so he left Toronto and signed on to coach the Winnipeg Jets. This year, new Toronto coach John Brophy once again brought the Leafs to within one game of the National Hockey League semifinal playoffs. And this time, just two days after the Leafs were eliminated by the Detroit Red Wings, Ballard gave his man a new three-year contract with what Brophy described as a "substantial" raise. Assistant coach Garry Lariviere of Gananoque also got a new three-year pact. 0 Pembroke Lumber Kings clinched a spot in the semifinal game of the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament last night with an 8-4 victory over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids. The Ontario champions will meet the loser of today's final round-robin game between Humboldt Broncos and Richmond Sockeyes, who both have 2-0 records. O Longueuil Chevaliers scored three goals in 47 seconds in the first period en route to a 10-4 triumph over Chicoutimi Sagueneens to win the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League championship last night. The Chevaliers, who won the best-of-seven series 4-1, will represent Quebec at the Memorial Cup tournament in Oshawa, which opens on Saturday.

Junior r Hockey League, opened with a loss to Humboldt Broncos in the best-of-seven Western Canada Centennial Cup championship, but win or lose, will be playing in the national four-team final. Humboldt, the Saskatchewan champs, scored twice in a 20-minute overtime period Monday night to earn a 5-3 victory in the opening game of the best-of-seven western final. However, as Humboldt will play host to the Canadian final - which also features a team from Ontario and one from the Atlantic provinces both Sockeyes and Broncos are guaranteed spots as the two Western representatives. by Alan Watson So close and yet . . . back 'to eight points out of a playoff position. A week in the B.C. Junior Hockey League which began so promising for the Abbotsford Falcons ended Sunday with the team back to realizing making up for a 1-9-1 start means only one thing hard work, and lots of it. Like, for the remaining 29 games of the regular season. Last Tuesday, the Falcons looked great in a 7-3 thumping of the Chilliwack Eagles. Saturday night at MSA Arena, they zoomed to a 3-1 first period lead against the second-place Richmond Sockeyes, but mental mistakes and costly turnovers resulted in an 8-4 setback. And Sunday in Juan de Fuca, against the Whalers who hold down the fourth and final playoff position in the Coastal Division, a tired Abbotsford team fell behind 6-2, battled back to 7-6 and lost it 8-6 after giving up an empty net goal. "We were enthused coming off the road and really pleased with our defensive game," said coach Jim Frost. "It seemed to carry over into the Chilliwack game and we played really well. It started us off well against Richmond, we made those mental mistakes and it hurt us. 1 It is disappointing." It didn't help matters that the Whalers went out and won three of four games to give themselves some breathing room, nor did it help matters that Sunday's game on the Island was Abbotsford's ninth in 17 days. However, Frost said the game plan is to carry on as usual. The team has improved drastically in the past month, and as Frost noted, "'We are so gol' dang close!" Against Richmond, the Sockeyes scored their first seven goals either shorthanded or on the powerplay, and Richmond coach Orland Kurtenback was breathing a little easier after watching his team snap a four-game losing streak. "We haven't been getting very good hockey out of our players," noted Kurtenbach. "We've been taking dumb penalties and getting ourselves in trouble. Tonight we got the individual efforts a team needs, which we hadn't been getting. We also played with a little cohesion, moved the puck and got some goals out of it."

BRONCOS Mike Gaber RICHMOND, SERIES| overtime Tuesday night to give the Humboldt Broncos a 4-3 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes and new life in their Centennial Cup playoff series. The victory forced a seventh and deciding game in the Western Canada final here Wednesday night. Bill McDougall led the Broncos with two goals and Curtis Chamberlain was the other marksman for Humboldt, which trailed 1-0 at the end of the first period and was tied 2-2 entering the third Paul led the periderford Sockeyes with two goals while Dave Tomlinson added the other goal for Richmond which was outshot 46-45. Humboldt, which got spectacular netminding from Bruce Hoffort who made 42 saves, scored twice on the power play while Richmond counted once with the man advantage. There were two key mistakes Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach felt gave Humboldt the victory. The first occurred seconds after Tomlinson scored to put the Sockeyes ahead 3-2 at 7:44 of the third period. Tony Bobbitt took a penalty for closing his hand on the puck and McDougall tied the game on the power play. The second incident came early in the 10-minute full-time overtime when Steve Jaques took a five-minute match penalty for -butting McDougall behind the play. McDougall picked up a holding minor on the play but the Broncos scored while the teams were playing even strength and then just kept the pressure on the Sockeyes with their power play. Jaques who was with the Broncos earlier this year faces at least a one suspension. •In Medicine Hat, Alta., Brian Gerrits scored at 15:23 of the first overtime period to lead the Portland Winter Hawks to a 3-2 win over the Medicine Hat Tigers in the second game of the Western Hockey League final.

... It's nice to see Nanaimo product Al Hill alive and well with Philadelphia Flyers in the playoffs. A lot of younger, people I've been talking sports with have never heard of Hill. He celebrated his 32nd birthday in game three by scoring the winner in Philly's 4-1 win over the Islanders. He then added another goal in game four Sunday, a 6-4 Flyer victory. Hill, who spent most of the year with Hershey Bears of the American League, collected 47 goals and 123 points in two seasons (1974-76) with Victoria Cougars. Ignored by the NHL, but drafted in 1975 by Indianapolis Racers of the World Hockey Association, Hill signed with the Flyers as a free agent and his best year with Philly came in 1979-80 when he counted 16 goals in 61 games. His biggest fan locally is his brother-in-law Jim Bayliss, who can be seen over the winter performing linseman duties at Clipper games. Greg Heakes of the Medicine Hat News tells me defenceman Wayne McBean is drawing high raves from NHL scouts as the June amateur lottery approaches. McBean has played both the powerplay and shorthanded situations as the Tigers have skated into the Western Hockey League finals against Portland Winter Hawks. David McNab, chief scout with Hartford Whalers, flew to Alberta in February for a private dinner meeting with McBean. "The idea of interviewing the kids is new," said Medicine Hat General Manager Russ Farwell. "They (scouts) are trying to judge a guy's character at 17. It is a shot in the dark for most teams.' McNab figures McBean will go in the top five picks, but Montreal Canadiens' Wilson wouldn't be as specific, saying McBean would be chosen in the first three rounds. New York Islander sniper Pat LaFontaine will this summer become the second New York hockey player to marry a pro model. LaFontaine will wed Marybeth Hopy, therefore joining Ranger Ron Greschner in model marriages, Greschner of course has been featured in every magazine there is along with his wife, super model Carol Alt. Humboldt Broncos had won 19 straight games until Richmond Sockeyes beat the Saskatchewan junior champions 5-3 Sunday night in Richmond. The Sockeyes have now played 27 post-season games and boast an impressive 21-6 record. You've got to wonder why the two clubs simply didn't play of series in Humboldt to decide the Abbott Cup. It would have made economical sense since both clubs are in the Centennial Cup tournament in Humboldt. Keith Gretzky appears to be doing alright in attempts to keep up with brother Wayne. The Hamilton Steelhawk forward was judged in the Ontario Hockey League coaches poll as being among the top two playmakers, smartest players and penalty killers.

The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos. Pembroke has a 0-2 record.
The payoff is a best-of-three series against the Richmond Sockeye's, champions of the B.C. Junior Hockey League.

By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate It's been a totally unpredictable season for the Red Deer Rustlers - they've lost games they were supposed to win and won at the most unexpected times. In other words, they've refused to play by the book. So why start now? Rustlers blew a chance to put the Richmond Sockeyes in cold storage Wednesday, falling 7-3 before 2,020 fans at the Red Deer Arena. The best-of-seven Western Centennial Cup semifinal series is now tied at 3-3, with the deciding game tonight at 7:30 p.m. at the Arena.

Scotty Morrison stepped off an airplane in Saskatoon late Thursday with one thing on his mind: "What were the scores in the hockey games tonight?" he asked of the first people he bumped into. After 23 years in the NHL's hièrarchy, Morrison still displays the interest of a fan when he talks about hockey, and he displays the same enthusiasm when he speaks about the state of the NHL in his official capacity as the league's vice-president of project development and president of the Hockey Hall of Fame. "The opportunities for a young person to get involved with the game have never been better." says Morrison, who will be the guest speaker at the Centennial Cup opening banquet in Humboldt this evening. "Whether someone is going to play Tier 2, go to university, be Olympic material and then on to professional, I don't think the opportunity has ever been better - not only as a player, but for people who want to be officials, or if they

could watch that replay 100 times and I still don't think you could find anything conclusive. "'The only way I'd like to see . them used is for the purpose of goal or no goal. For instance, in the Montreal-Quebec game there was no question that puck went in, so that shouldn't fall under the criteria of the instant replay. But checking the goal or no goal is one more assist for the referee and he should welcome it. "Those ones that go just under the crossbar or just across the line and the goaltender pulls it out, sometimes the referee can't see them. There are times when it looks like the official is standing there looking at it, but it takes just a split-second for. somebody to skate i in front of him and maybe he misses it. He has the appeal of the goal judge and his linesman, but if he could just look at the replay once and either stand by his decision or change it, replays might be helpful." Tickets to hear Morrison speak at the Centennial Cup banquet, which will be held at the Uniplex, are $25 and can be purchased at the Bronco office.

who had been playing AAA midget hockey for the Valley Wildcats in Greenwood, N.S. The Fuel Kids offered Spencer $1,000 towards room and board. The straight A student is being considered for a scholarship to Princeton University in New Jersey. After that, four more players were given money for room and board. The team also found fulltime employment for Scott ! MacTavish and Chris Conrad and part-time jobs for seven. other players. "We decided we needed a recruiting package to become competitive. We try to rely on the quality, of the (Dartmouth) Sportsplex and the very good coaching staff. But sometimes, something more is needed." It costs the Fuel Kids about $2,000 a year to keep each player in equipment. The Fuel Kids' executive is hoping money from a summer hockey camp will off their outstanding debts. They're also hoping for some profit from the Centennial Cup. Four teams are competing for the Cup - the Fuel Kids, Humboldt Broncos, Pembroke, Ont., Lumber Kings and the Richmond, B.C., Sockeyes. Weanus said the teams will be allowed to split any money left after series expenses are paid.

OTTAWA (CP) - With four finalists for the tier two Centennial Cup junior hockey final decided, the governing Canadian Amateur Hockey Association announced Thursday the playoff schedule for the roundrobin series in Humboldt. Starting Saturday, Atlantic champion Dartmouth, N.S., Fuel Kids open against the host Humboldt Broncos. On Sunday, Richmond, B.C., Sockeyes play Dartmouth, and Humboldt meets the Pembroke, Ont., Lumber Kings. The round-robin concludes with Pembroke against Richmond on Monday, Dartmouth playing Pembroke on Tuesday and Richmond meeting Humboldt on Wednesday. Based on the outcome of these games, the third- and second-placed teams meet in a semi-final on Thursday. The winner goes against the round-robin winner in the championship final on Saturday, May 9.

DARTMOUTH, N.S. (CP) It's been a good year for Richard Weanus, president of the Dartmouth Fuel Kids. His junior A hockey team has made it into the national Centennial Cup championships, scheduled to start Saturday in Humboldt. And the Fuel Kids are only $10,000 to $15,000 in debt. "There's a lot of relief now," Weanus said in an interview this week. "Last year, this time we were $30,000 in the red." Most people have no idea, Weanus said, of how much it costs to operate a junior A team in Canada. The Fuel Kids, who defeated Charlottetown Abbies last week to advance to the national championship, have operated in the past on annual budgets of $50,000 $55,000. "*Most of that money went to transportation, ice costs and equipment," Weanus said. *Those are the three big expenses.' This year. the team's budget soared to $80,000. "Part of that increase was due to a jump ice costs, travel costs and refereeing, Weanus said. "But it was also due to a recruiting cost - something we didn't have before this Christmas." HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Humboldt Broncos won a berth in the final of the Centennial Cup junior 'A' hockey championship tournament Wednesday night with a 6-1 victory over Richmond Sockeyes. The losers, with a round-robin record of 2- 1, will meet Pembroke Lumber Kings in tonight's semifinal. The final will be played Saturday Bill McDougall and Garnett Kazuik led Humboldt with two goals apiece, while Duncon Ryhorchuk and Curtis Chamberlin fired singles. The win gave Humboldt a 3-0 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament. Stan Czenczek scored the Richmond goal. The teams were tied 1-1 after the first period, but Humboldt led 4-1 after the second. The Broncos outshot the B.C.-champion Sockeyes 37-19. Humboldt take 15 of 27 minors. Both teams took two five-minute majors and two game misconducts. Humbolt also picked up two 10-minute misconducts. The Lumber Kings, from Ontario, finished with a 1-2 record in the round-robin section of the tournament. The fourth team involved, Dartmouth Fuel Kids from Nova Scotia, lost all three of its games.

Richmond 9 Pembroke 3 HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Richmond Sockeyes whipped Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a semifinal clash Thursday night to advance to the final game in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey tournament. The Sockeyes, from British Columbia, will meet the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in the sudden-death final Saturday night. Richmond, which made it to the semifinal with a 2-1 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament, received goals from Dean Rutledge, Steve Jaques, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, Jason Phillips, Tony
1975 - Ed Staniowski, Regina CENTENNIAL CUP Richmond 9 Pembroke 3 (Sudden -death semifinal)

HUMBOLDT What was expected to be the closest, tightestchecking, most-exciting game of the Centennial Cup round robin ed, as Humboldt Broncos turned into one of the most-lopsid- 0 hammered Richmond Sockeyes 6- 1 to advance to the Centennial Cup final. It was the first time in eight games between the two Western finalists that either team has registered what could be labelled a blowout. Not only did the Broncos outscore Richmond, they dominated the Sockeyes in every aspect of the game. Richmond, which finished the round robin at 2-1, will meet Pembroke, 1-2, in the semi-final tonight at 8:30 at the Uniplex. The winner will play the Broncos, 3-0, in the Centennial Cup championship game Saturday at 7:30 p.m. "We got outworked, outplayed, out-everythinged," said RichKurtenbach. "It's not as if we weren't trying. The 'oomph' just wasn't there.' If their was any 'oomph' in the Sockeyes at the start of the game, the Broncos were determined to knock it out of them. Right from the opening faceoff, Humboldt came out hitting, levelling several Sockeyes with solid bodychecks in the opening minutes. "A hit is almost like a goal sometimes,' said Bronco defenceman Al Novakowski, who has scored more of the former than the latter. "A big hit can really settle the other team down. That's what happened tonight." Richmond stayed close to the Broncos in the first period, when the teams traded power-play goals. Bill McDougall pounced on a rebound to open the scoring for the Broncos at 6:17, but Stan Czenczek tied the game with a hard slapshot that beat netminder Bruce Hoffort at 17:26. After that, however, it was all Humboldt. Garnet Kazuik started the ball rolling with a shorthanded goal at 13:20 of the second period, stealing the puck from Steve Jaques at the Richmond blueline to have a clear breakaway on Jamie Stewart.
