By ELLIOTT PAP San Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Dressed in their black uniforms, Richmond Sockeyes look every bit the villains they are turning out to be in this year's Centennial Cup tournament. The fans in Humboldt cheer against them and the referees don't seem to appreciate their somewhat rugged style. So all coach Orland Kurtenbach's boys do is win, win, win. The 'Eyes polished off Ontario champion Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night to go 2-0 in the round-robin and clinch a semifinal berth in the national junior A hockey championships. Richmond has today off and will meet the host Humboldt Broncos, who are also 2-0, Wednesday. At stake will be a bye into Saturday's final, while the loser will meet either Pembroke or Atlantic champion Dartmouth in Thursday's semi-final. Tonight's match sees 0-2 Pembroke and 0-2 Dartmouth squaring off for the remaining playoff spot. The Sockeyes were too much for the Lumber Kings last night despite spending half the game in the penalty box. Referee Wes Smith of Saskatoon called all the borderline stuff - with Richmond committing most of it - and that left Kurtenbach in a state of near apoplexy. "All I'm asking for is a fair shake from the refereeing," said Kurten-

THE MAN who checked in at the registration desk at the downtown Vancouver hotel asked if he could get the Canadiens-Nordiques game on his television set. The clerk replied in the negative. "Well, then maybe I'll take in the RichmondHumboldt game. It should be a heck'uva series," he commented. He knew the teams would be returning to Humboldt to play for the Centennial Cup and hoped to take in at least one of the games. I happened to be on holidays at the time and the next day planned to be in Kelowna. At Arby's, I ran into big Gene McWillie. I didn't realize he was in Kelowna with the Canadian men's national softball team preparing for the Pan-American Games later this summer and the World Softball championship to be held in Saskatoon in 1988. So, as well as taking in the doubleheader that night, I also got a chance for a short interview with the team's manager, Dave Pearce of Regina.

team played in the national finals. In Humboldt, the Broncos have a good shot at winning the Centennial Cup. In Medicine Hat, the Blades came closer than they have in years at a shot at the Memorial Cup. The Hilltops once again played in the Prairie Junior Football Conference finals. Reigning Canadian five-pin champ Blair Pizzey of Saskatoon won the $25,000-CBC championship in Toronto. Blaine Sands of Tisdale won a gold medal in the Canadian senior boxing championships in New Brunswick. In less than two weeks, eight Saskatoon athletes will be competing in the Canadian women's gymnastics events in 1 Winnipeg. These athletes have put Saskatchewan on the map. And during the next three years, Saskatoon will be the place on the map that will be circled.

"That third goal put the crimpers on it," said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach. "I told them before the third period that we've had a series of mountains to climb: some of them were smaller, but they've all been tough to climb. We had one last one to go in the third period and they haven't failed me all year. In the stress situations, they've come through like gangbusters." While Kurtenbach celebrated the championship in his first year with Richmond, Lynch, who could move on to major. junior hockey next season, was left to reflect on his record with the Broncos. "We're 156-29 in the last two years," said Lynch, "but couldn't win the game we wanted to win. It's bitter. "But we've started something and we've got something to build on here. We played hard to the end of the game, and the guys who are coming back will know what it takes to get to the Centennial Cup and they'll make sure the rookies coming in will learn what it takes. You build on that. It's bitter and disappointing, but the future certainly isn't bleak for this organization. If anything, it's extremely bright." Tournament tidbits: Saturday's final drew a standing-room-only crowd of around 2,400. • Richmond and Humboldt played nine times in three weeks, with the Sockeyes winning five times and each team scoring 30 goals. "If we played them again tomorrow, we'd win and the series would continue,' said Lynch.. . Richmond took seven of 12 minor penalties. . Pembroke . Lumber Kings, who lost to Richmond in the semi-final, will play host to next year's Cen-
Sockeyes win Centennial al Cup

HUMBOLDT - Frank Romeo looked like a walking trophy shop as he ambled into the champagne war going on in the Richmond Sockeyes' dressing g room following their 5-2 Centennial Cup-clinching win over Humboldt Broncos at the Uniplex on Saturday. With a plaque for MVP of the final game under one arm, a trophy as tournament MVP under the other, and the Centennial Cup firmly in his grasp, Romeo surveyed the scene with wide eyes, saying with a grin: "This is unbelievable. I'm the happiest guy in the world. Winning the Cup, being MVP, it's like Christmas all over again." If this was the holiday season, the Humboldt shooters would be calling the Sockeye Richmond 46-23, but stingy Romeo was netminder Scrooge. The, Broncos outshot giving away nothing. It was the third time Romeo was named player of the game during the tournament, winning the award in each of his starts. "I said to him when we were shaking hands, 'Frank, you know you really played over your head this tournament and that's what you've got to do to be great,' " said a disappointed Bernie Lynch, coach of the Broncos. "I told him he came up really big and played great in the tournament and that's the sign of a great hockey player. That's what you've got to do to be a champion." Richmond also received an -winning performance from Jason 1 Phillips, who was selected the tournament's most gentlemanly player. The speedy winger scored

Sockeyes gain final in Centennial Cup

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - The Richmond Sockeyes whipped the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 Thursday to advance to Saturday's Centennial Cup Tier II Junior A hockey final against the Humboldt Broncos. Richmond scorers were Dean Rutledge, Steve Jaques, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, Jason Phillips, Tony Bobbitt, Mike McCormick and Bill Hardy.

to attend the 60th anniversary fastball reunion here Aug. 28, 29 and 30. . .Rollie also informs that former Sault Ste. Marie hockey star Jim Farelli is doing a fine job of coaching the Pembroke Lumber Kings, who are on the trail of the Centennial Cup. .Jim is a nephew of former NHL referee Lou Farelli of North Bay.. Speaking of that fastball reunion, if Tony Poeta's sporting goods store looks more like a post office lately, it's because he's been in charge of mailing out invitations to the big get-together. .Replies are already starting to come in. . Former North Bay athlete Bus Wall phones from the Sault to correct me on a hockey forward line on which he played. . .His linemates were Adam (Perly) Finnigan and Rolly Benoit. did play with Don Richmond and Bill Ringler," says Bus, "but that was in bantam hockey.' . .The other line was a junior trio. .Wall also plans to attend the fastball reunion. .He also asked this writer to pass on regards to Dr. Stan Brown from Curly Keenan of the Sault. . .Keenan remembers when Stan played for the 1924 Allan Cup-wining Sault Greyhounds.. Sockeyes the men in black in Centennial Cup tourney

By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Dressed in their black uniforms, Richmond Sockeyes look every bit the villains they are turning out to be in this year's Centennial Cup tournament. The fans in Humboldt cheer against them and the referees don't seem to appreciate their somewhat rugged style. So all coach Orland Kurtenbach's boys do is win, win, win. The 'Eyes polished off Ontario champion Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night to go 2-0 in the round-robin and clinch a semifinal berth in the national junior A hockey championships. Richmond has today o off and will meet the host Humboldt Broncos, who are also 2-0, Wednesday. At stake will be a bye into Saturday's final, while the loser will meet either Pembroke or Atlantic champion Dartmouth in Thursday's semi-final. Tonight's match sees 0-2 Pembroke and 0-2 Dartmouth squaring off for the remaining playoff spot. The Sockeyes were too much for the Lumber Kings last night despite spending half the game in the penalty box. Referee Wes Smith of Saskatoon called all the borderline stuff - with Richmond committing most of it and that left Kurten- bach in a state of near apoplexy. "All I'm asking for is a fair shake from the refereeing," said Kurten-

read sports sections of the major daily newspapers. At least one newspaper report suggested the story was the broken Can- in an attempt to demoralize adiens in their series against the Nordiques. EAST RUTHERFORD, N.J. (AP) Rumors that Providence College would be losing someone from its athletic department to the New York metropolitan area turned out to be on target - almost. However, the move wasn't the one everyone expected. Rick Pitino remained the Friars' head basketball coach. It was athletic director Lou Lamoriello who said goodbye Thursday to Providence and became president of the New Jersey Devils. No contract terms were disclosed. Lamoriello, 44 replaces Bob Butera, who resigned last Friday but remained with the NHL club as a consultant. •... OTTAWA (CP) - With four finalists for the tier two Centennial Cup junior hockey final decided, the governing Canadian Amateur Hockey Association announced Thursday the playoff schedule for the round-robin series in Humboldt, Sask. Starting Saturday, Atlantic champion Dartmouth, N.S., Fuel Kids open against the host Humboldt. Cup matchups announced The Canadian Amateur Hockey Association announced the playoff matchups for the tier two Centennial Cup junior hockey finals yesterday. The Atlantic-champion Dartmouth Fuel Kids open against the home club from Humboldt, Sask. tomorrow. The Richmond B. C. Sockeyes play Dartmouth Sunday while Humboldt meets the Lumber Kings from Pembroke, Ont. The roundrobin concludes with Pembroke against Richmond Monday, Dartmouth versus Pembroke Tuesday and Richmond meeting Humboldt Wednesday.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Pembroke Lumber Kings, on the strength of five power play goals, trounced Dartmouth Fuel Kids 8-4 Tuesday night to advance to the Centennial Cup playoff round. The Ontario champions finished the round-robin portion of the national Junior A hockey championships at 1-2 while Dartmouth was winless at 0-3. Pembroke will meet the loser of tonight's first-place showdown between Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos in Thursday's semi-final. The RichmondHumboldt winner receives a bye into Saturday night's championship 1 final.

Senior Women's Championship Semi-finals

DARTMOUTH, N.S. (CP) - It's been a good year for Richard Weanus, president of the Dartmouth Fuel Kids. His junior A hockey team has made it into the national Centennial Cup championships, scheduled to start Saturday in Humboldt, Sask. And the Fuel Kids are only $10,000 to $15,000 in debt. "There's a lot of relief now," he said in an interview this week. "Last year, this time we were $30,000 in the red." Most people have no idea, Weanus said, of how much it costs to operate a junior A team in Canada. The Fuel Kids, who defeated Charlottetown Abbies last week to advance to the national championship, have operated in the past on annual budgets of $50,000 to $55,000. "Most of that money went to transportation, ice costs and equipment," Weanus said. "Those are the three big expenses." This year, the team's budget soared to $80,000. "Part of that increase was due to a jump in ice costs, travel costs and refereeing," Weanus said. "But it was also due to a recruitcost something we didn't have before this Christmas.' The team took its first step into recruiting when it went after John Spencer, a high-school student who played AAA midget hockey for the Valley Wildcats in Greenwood, N.S. The Fuel Kids offered Spencer $1,000 towards room and board. The straight A student is being considered for a scholarship to Princeton University in New Jer-

After that, four more players were given money for room and board. The team also found fulltime employment for Scott MacTaVishe and Chris Conrad and partjobs for seven other players. "We decided we needed a recruiting package to become competitive. We try to rely on the quality of the (Dartmouth) Sportsplex and the very good coaching staff. But sometimes, something more is needed." It costs the Fuel Kids about $2,000 a year to keep each player in equipment. The Fuel Kids' executive is hoping money from a summer hockey camp will pay off their outstanding debts. They're also hoping for some profit from the Centennial Cup. Four teams are competing for the Cup - the Fuel Kids, the Humboldt Broncos, the Pembroke, Ont., Lumber Kings and the Richmond, B.C., Sockeyes. Weanus the teams will be allowed to split any money left after series expenses are paid. The Fuel Kids could be entering the series without their star centre, Jerry Scott. Friday, May 8, 1987 Saskatoon, Saskatchewan Star-Phoenix Sports B13 It's Sockeyes and Broncos in Centennial Cup final

The two prize fighters of Junior A Tier II hockey will meet for the ninth time in less than three weeks, this time with the Centennial Cup championship on the line, at 7:30 p.m. on Saturday. Richmond Sockeyes earned the chance to settle their grudge series with Humboldt Broncos by defeating the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in the semi-final on Thursday at the Uniplex.

"If they had won, I wouldn't have wanted to shake hands with them either. We knew we had to play each other again in the Centennial Cup; it's not like we wanted to be buddies with them.' -Richmond's Steve Jaques

With a horde of college and professional scouts attending the Centennial Cup, several more players will likely be recruited this week.

"I don't blame them," said Jaques. "If they had won, I wouldn't have wanted to shake hands with them either. We knew we had to play each other again in the Centennial Cup; it's not like we wanted to be buddies with them.'

Centennial Cup victory for Richmond HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo was named most valuable player in the four-team, series. British Columbia's Sockeyes robin skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2-1 before Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond, which took seven of the game's 12 minor penalties. Curtis Chamberlin and Kevin Luke scored for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos.

Sockeyes sock it to Broncos with Centennial Cup victory

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net.

The roundrobin 1 concludes with Pembroke against Richmond Monday, Dartmouth versus Pembroke Tuesday and Richmond meeting Humboldt Wednesday.

OTTAWA (CP) - With four finalists for the Centennial Cup junior hockey final decided, the governing Canadian Amateur Hockey Association announced Thursday the playoff schedule for the round-robin series in Humboldt. Starting Saturday at 7:30 p.m., Atlantic champion Dartmouth, N.S., Fuel Kids open against the host Humboldt Broncos. Belle Buttons present skit at lodge in JC JUNCTION CITY - The Eagles Lodge No. 830, Junction City, honored a group of Herington Centennial members Saturday evening. A Centennial Belles group, the Belle Buttons, put on a skit. They sang their theme song and rang their bells, singing the "Kansas Waltz," composed by Teen Donahue. Those participating in the skit were Rosie Girton, Georgia Robison, Karen Donahue, Mary Ann Brown, Bobbi Ann Johnson, Beulah Vanderslice, Clyde Shamburg and Ross Girton. Bob Roberts, dressed as Uncle Sam, distributed centennial flyers and Harry Robison distributed song sheets. Acting mayor, Vance Donahue, presented a Herington Centennial Cup to the president of the Eagles Lodge, Clint Gfeller, and invited Junction Citians to attend the Centennial celebration. Others attending from Herington were Estelle Baker, Verna Warneke, Mr. and Mrs. Glen Fudge. Out-of-town people with the Herington group were Mr. and Mrs. Bill (Sybil Schlesener) Rock, of Milford; Estelle Baker's family, Mr. and Mrs. C.J. Baker of Salina, Mr. and Mrs. Bob Thompson and Barbara Muller of Wichita; Mr. and Mrs. Earl Higgins of Abilene; Mr. and Mrs. Herman McNeil, Mr. and Mrs. George Trow, all of Salina, Carroll Gates and Clyde Shamburg, both of Glasco.

Sockeyes built for success By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - The timehonored theory that you can't build a winner overnight has taken a kick in the butt from the Richmond Sockeyes. The newly crowned western junior A hockey champions are a team for the present. Forget last year, forget next year. The future is now. Centennial Cup, here we come. A year ago, the 'Eyes captured the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Coastal Division title. But when training camp opened last fall, there were just two returning players - Jim Gunn and goalie Glenn Pencer. It wasn't so much a housecleaning as a demolition. The man behind it all is owner Bruce Taylor, a Burnaby roofing contractor. "Two years ago I was with the Burnaby Bluehawks, and we won the Coastal Division but lost the league final," says Taylor. "Last year we won the Coastal and we lost the league final. You learn a little something along the way. I think we've learned what it takes to win a Centennial Cup." The Sockeyes begin the final

"When the Humboldt Broncos were in Selkirk (playing the Centennial Cup quarter-finals against the Manitoba Junior Hockey League-champion Steelers) it was interesting to note they all wore face shields. I sat with an official from the SJHL (Saskatchewan Amateur Junior Hockey League) and asked him what the reaction was from their making that compulsory, and he said there was no reaction at all. He said they just wore them.' The Saskatchewan official told Allard that the rule had resulted in a 'substantial reduction' in dental accidents. The MAHA last year paid $105,000 in insurance premiums for some 27,000 members. "'That's a lot of money," said Allard. "Making (a face protection rule) compulsory would certainly result in a reduction in dental accidents, which is where most of the claims are filed against.'

HUMBOLDT - Question: If Humboldt Broncos and Richmond Sockeyes were willing to battle each other like life-long enemies throughout a seven-game series which meant nothing, what will they do in a single game to advance to the Centennial Cup final? Richmond and Humboldt will meet in the final game of the Centennial Cup round robin at the Uniplex on Wednesday, with the winner earning an automatic berth in the championship game Saturday night. The loser will face either Pembroke Lumber Kings or Dartmouth Fuel Kids in the semi-final on Thursday.

"Those other teams had to be champions to get here, but we know who we have to beat to win the Centennial Cup," said Richmond defenceman Steve Jaques, after the Sockeyes defeated Pembroke 4-1 on Monday to pull even with Humboldt at 2-0 in the Centennial Cup standings. "It'll be a big game on Wednesday.' The Sockeyes needed two thirdperiod goals from Dave Tomlinson to stop the Lumber Kings, who fell to 0-2 in the tournament. Pembroke will play Dartmouth, also 0-2 at 7:30 tonight. "We just hung in for two periods while we killed all those penalties," said Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach, whose team

"Everybody has a job to do," explained Lynch. "The forwards are watching their forwards, the defenceman watch their defenceman and the goalies watch their goalies. We feel that instead of letting them sit in the stands with their girlfriends or parents, they should do the scouting. It's their tournament; why not let them show some initiative to help themselves win it." What the Broncos might have seen was almost a mirror image of themselves. Both teams have a handful of talented offensive players and a host of laborers. In the Centennial Cup tournament both teams have beaten Pembroke and Dartmouth with a combined goals-for-and-against of 11-4.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Named to the Centennial Cup hockey tournament all-star team, which was selected before semifinal play, were three Humboldt players, two from Richmond and one from Pembroke. Humboldt's Bill McDougall, who tied for the round-robin scoring lead with four goals and three assists in three games, was the choice at centre along with teammates Rob Rice, a burly defenceman who hails from Prince Edward Island, and right wing Duncan Ryhorchuk. Getting the nod from Richmond were left wing Jason Phillips, second in round-robin scoring with three goals and three, assists, and defenceman Matt Hervey. By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Since the Richmond Sockeyes and the Humboldt Broncos first began tangling, all the talk has been of how the two junior A hockey clubs are so evenly matched. Now, suddenly, they're both underdogs - against each other in the Centennial Cup Canadian championship. Confusing? You bet.

VIENNA (CP) - Saying Canadians never can get enough of good hockey, Al Eagleson, the organizer of the Canada Cup tournament, has released a schedule of 34 games to be played this summer among the best players in the world. Team Canada opens camp in Montreal on Aug. 3. There are 14 exhibition games in the Canada Cup schedule and 20 tournament games, if the final goes the maximum three games. Hamilton gets 10 games. The six-team round-robin tournament begins Aug. 28 and ends Sept. 6. The playoffs are scheduled to begin Sept. 8. "We think Hamilton will do well," Eagleson said at a news conference Thursday. "It's going to be a big test for the Hamilton market." Tickets will be costly.
